title: Linear Regression: Your First Real Prediction
learningObjectives: Explain the difference between a feature (input) and a target (what you're predicting)
Split data into train and test sets and explain why evaluating on training data is misleading
Fit a scikit-learn LinearRegression model and generate predictions
Interpret a model's coefficients in terms of the real-world quantities they represent
whyItMatters: Every hour of theory in Mission 1 and 2 built the feature table; this is the lesson where that table finally produces its first actual prediction — the 'Predict' layer of the AI Energy OS starts here, with the simplest model that can possibly work.
capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's first predictive model — a linear regression baseline predicting demand_kw from temperature and hour_of_day, the simplest member of the forecasting family this mission builds toward.

problem.summary: The feature table exists, but nothing yet predicts tomorrow's demand from it.
problem.content: You have a feature table with temperature, hour_of_day, and historical demand_kw — but nothing yet that actually predicts tomorrow's demand from those features.

motivation.summary: 'Predict the Future' becomes literally true in code for the first time here.
motivation.content: This is the exact moment the mission's name — 'Predict the Future' — becomes literally true in code, for the first time. Everything before this point prepared the data; this is where a model uses it to say something about what hasn't happened yet.

theory.summary: Split into train/test; fit on train; coefficients are directly interpretable.
theory.content: Supervised learning splits data into features (X, the inputs — temperature, hour_of_day) and a target (y, what you're predicting — demand_kw). `train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.2)` holds out a portion of data the model never sees during training, so evaluating on it approximates how the model will perform on genuinely new data — evaluating on training data instead is misleading because the model may have simply memorized it. `LinearRegression().fit(X_train, y_train)` learns a coefficient per feature; `model.coef_` shows how much predicted demand changes per unit change in each feature, holding others constant — directly interpretable, unlike many more complex models.

visualExplanation.summary: A fitted line through a scatter plot; train/test shown as non-overlapping regions.
visualExplanation.content: A scatter of temperature vs. demand points with a fitted regression line through them sits alongside a diagram showing the train/test split as two non-overlapping colored regions of the same dataset.

interactiveDemo.summary: Drag points and watch the fitted line update; toggle train/test coloring.
interactiveDemo.content: Drag data points on a scatter plot and watch a live-refitting regression line update, then toggle which points are 'test' vs. 'train' and see the line only ever fit using the train-colored points.

guidedCoding.summary: Follow along: fit a linear regression and inspect its coefficients.
guidedCoding.content:
```python
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split

X = combined[["temp_c", "hour_of_day"]]
y = combined["demand_kw"]
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.2, shuffle=False)

model = LinearRegression()
model.fit(X_train, y_train)
predictions = model.predict(X_test)
print(model.coef_, model.intercept_)
```

independentCoding.summary: Fit and interpret a regression predicting solar output, unaided.
independentCoding.content: Fit a linear regression predicting `solar_output_kw` from `temp_c` and a `cloud_cover` feature, and interpret whether each coefficient's sign (positive/negative) matches physical intuition.

debugging.summary: Fix an evaluation that scores against the training set instead of the test set.
debugging.content: Code evaluates model performance by scoring against `X_train`/`y_train` instead of the held-out test set, producing a misleadingly optimistic result. Identify and fix the evaluation to use the test set.

reflection.summary: Training-set performance is optimistically biased even without a coding mistake.
reflection.content: Why would a model's performance on its own training data almost always look better than its real-world performance, even without anything going wrong in the code?

teachBack.summary: Explain what model.coef_ represents using your own fitted model.
teachBack.content: Explain to someone what `model.coef_` actually represents, using the temperature coefficient from your own fitted model as a concrete example.

miniProject.summary: Fit and interpret a demand regression with plain-English coefficient explanations.
miniProject.content: Fit and interpret a linear regression predicting `demand_kw` from the features in your Mission 2 feature table, reporting both the coefficients and a plain-English sentence explaining what each one means physically.

portfolioEvidence.summary: This is the Digital Twin's first working predictive model.
portfolioEvidence.content: This is the Digital Twin's first working predictive model — the simplest possible member of the forecasting family the rest of this mission builds on.

labs.title: First Fit
labs.description: Sandboxed exercise fitting a LinearRegression model on a provided feature table, requiring correct train/test evaluation and coefficient interpretation.

simulations.title: Regression Line Fit Visualizer
simulations.description: Live-refitting regression line over a draggable scatter plot, with toggleable train/test point coloring.

flashcards.front: Why is evaluating a model on its own training data misleading?
flashcards.back: The model may have partially memorized the training data rather than learned a generalizable pattern, so its training-set performance tends to look better than its real-world performance on unseen data.

flashcards.front: What does a LinearRegression model's coef_ for a given feature represent?
flashcards.back: How much the predicted target changes per one-unit change in that feature, holding all other features constant — directly interpretable in the units of the original data.

rubric: Correctly identifies and fixes evaluation performed on training data instead of the held-out test set
Coefficient interpretation is expressed in terms of real-world units and direction, not just the raw number
Independent-coding task assesses whether coefficient signs match physical intuition, not just whether the code runs
